ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fish ball entry (FOODON_03315595); no specific entry exists for fried fish balls with curry sauce. Closest parent used.

allergens: gluten (fish ball binders may contain wheat starch)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried fish ball curry consists of 5 pieces, weighing approximately 100 g, served as a snack or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Ball, Fried — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for fried fish balls. Curry sauce contribution is estimated by adding typical curry sauce macros (approximately 5–8 kcal per 100 g from spice paste and oil) to the base fried fish ball values. The dominant nutrient profile is from the fried fish ball component. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo fried fish ball entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for fish balls (SR Legacy) to confirm plausibility.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fish-Based Products and Analogous Estimates — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried fish balls with curry sauce. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous fish-based processed products in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Fish balls are predominantly protein and fat with moderate starch from binders; deep-frying and protein matrix slow glucose absorption, supporting a low GI estimate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 11.2) / 100 ≈ 4.5 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of fried fish ball curry due to its high sodium content (~520 mg/100 g), which can contribute significantly to daily sodium intake; portion control and choosing lower-sodium curry preparations are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content with low glycemic index (~40)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried fish ball curry has a low estimated glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content (~11 g/100 g), making it a relatively acceptable option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions, though the fat content from deep-frying warrants attention for overall caloric management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and fat from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~520 mg/100 g) and fat from deep-frying (total fat ~9.8 g/100 g, saturated fat ~2.4 g/100 g) makes fried fish ball curry a moderate concern for cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency and portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Fish Ball Curry (咖喱炸鱼蛋, Bebola Ikan Goreng Kari) is a popular Singaporean Chinese snack and side dish consisting of deep-fried fish balls coated or served with a spiced curry sauce. Fish balls are made from minced fish paste — typically made from wolf herring, yellow croaker, or other white fish — mixed with starch binders, salt, and seasoning, then shaped into spheres and deep-fried until golden and slightly crispy on the outside while remaining springy and tender inside. The curry sauce is a fragrant blend of curry spices, chilli, coconut milk or oil, and aromatics such as lemongrass and galangal, lending the dish a bold, spicy, and savoury flavour profile. This dish is commonly sold at hawker centres, food courts, and zi char stalls across Singapore, and is often served as a snack or accompaniment to rice or noodles. Nutritionally, fried fish balls provide a moderate source of protein (~11.5 g/100 g) and are energy-dense due to deep-frying (~175 kcal/100 g). The sodium content is notably high (~520 mg/100 g), which is a clinical concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is low (~40), making it relatively suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderation. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker contexts and is dairy-free, though it may contain gluten from starch binders used in fish ball production.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo fried fish ball data and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy fish ball entries. No exact match for fried fish balls with curry sauce exists in any single approved source; values represent a defensible adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar fish-based processed products. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy entries for fried fish balls. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this food. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this food. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this food. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this food. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this food. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this food. The input record described the serving as '5pcs' from Hai Kah Lang at Funan Mall (GrabFood Singapore); this commercial context is preserved in the serving description. Price data (SGD 10.49) is retained from the input for reference but is not used in nutritional analysis. The curry sauce component adds spice-derived micronutrients and marginal additional fat and sodium; these are incorporated into the adapted per-100g values. GI is estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous fish-based processed products from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in approved sources.
